question_en: Who painted the Mona Lisa (La Gioconda)?
context_en: Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between 1503 and 1519. It is the most famous work in the world, kept at the Louvre in Paris.

question_en: Who sculpted 'David'?
context_en: Michelangelo sculpted David between 1501 and 1504. It stands 5.17 m tall and is carved from a single block of Carrara marble.

question_en: In which city is the Prado Museum?
context_en: The Prado is in Madrid and is one of the most important museums in the world, with works by Velázquez, Goya, El Greco and Rubens.

question_en: Who composed the Fifth Symphony?
context_en: Ludwig van Beethoven composed his Fifth Symphony in C minor (1808). The famous opening motif (ta-ta-ta-TA) is one of the most recognizable in classical music.

question_en: Who wrote 'Don Quixote'?
context_en: Cervantes published the first part of Don Quixote in 1605 and the second in 1615. It is considered the first modern novel and the most influential in Spanish literature.

question_en: Who painted 'Guernica'?
context_en: Picasso painted Guernica in 1937 as a protest against the bombing of the Basque city during the Spanish Civil War. It is in the Reina Sofía Museum, Madrid.

question_en: Which architect designed the Sagrada Familia in Barcelona?
context_en: Antoni Gaudí designed the Sagrada Familia in 1883, a project still unfinished. It is the most recognizable architectural symbol of Barcelona.

question_en: Who wrote 'Hamlet'?
context_en: Shakespeare wrote Hamlet around 1600-1601. 'To be or not to be' is the most famous soliloquy in world literature.

question_en: Who painted 'Las Meninas'?
context_en: Diego Velázquez painted Las Meninas in 1656. It depicts the Infanta Margarita surrounded by her maids in the Alcázar of Madrid. It is in the Prado.

question_en: Who composed 'The Magic Flute'?
context_en: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart composed The Magic Flute in 1791, the year of his death. It is one of the most performed operas in the world.

question_en: Which artistic movement did Salvador Dalí lead?
context_en: Dalí was the leading figure of Surrealism. 'The Persistence of Memory' (1931), with its soft clocks, is his most iconic work.

question_en: Who wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'?
context_en: García Márquez published 'One Hundred Years of Solitude' in 1967. It is the pinnacle work of magical realism and earned him the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982.

question_en: Which architect designed the Bilbao Guggenheim?
context_en: Frank Gehry designed the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao (1997), considered the most important building of the 20th century. Its titanium reflects light in changing ways.

question_en: Who painted 'The Starry Night'?
context_en: Van Gogh painted 'The Starry Night' in 1889 from the Saint-Rémy asylum. It is one of the most recognized paintings in the world.

question_en: Who sculpted 'The Thinker'?
context_en: Auguste Rodin created 'The Thinker' in 1880, originally as part of 'The Gates of Hell'. There are multiple copies around the world.

question_en: Who composed 'The Four Seasons'?
context_en: Antonio Vivaldi composed 'The Four Seasons' around 1718-1720. They are four violin concertos representing the seasons of the year.

question_en: Which writer created the character of Sherlock Holmes?
context_en: Arthur Conan Doyle created Sherlock Holmes in 1887. The detective lives at 221B Baker Street, London, with his friend Dr. Watson.

question_en: Who painted 'The Garden of Earthly Delights'?
context_en: Bosch painted this triptych in the late 15th century, depicting Paradise, carnal sin and Hell. It is in the Prado Museum.

question_en: Who wrote '1984'?
context_en: George Orwell published '1984' in 1949. It is a dystopia about a totalitarian state with Big Brother watching everything. He coined terms like 'doublethink'.

question_en: In which museum is the Venus de Milo?
context_en: The Venus de Milo is a Greek sculpture from the 2nd century BC, found in 1820. It is in the Louvre in Paris. Its author is unknown.

question_en: Who composed 'La Traviata'?
context_en: Giuseppe Verdi composed 'La Traviata' in 1853, based on Dumas's 'The Lady of the Camellias'. It is one of the most performed operas in the world.

question_en: Which writer created Alice in Wonderland?
context_en: Lewis Carroll (Charles Dodgson) published 'Alice's Adventures in Wonderland' in 1865. It is one of the most translated books in history.

question_en: Who directed 'The Godfather'?
context_en: Francis Ford Coppola directed 'The Godfather' in 1972, with Marlon Brando and Al Pacino. It won the Oscar for Best Picture and is considered one of the best in history.

question_en: Who painted 'The Birth of Venus'?
context_en: Botticelli painted 'The Birth of Venus' around 1485. It is in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence. It depicts the goddess Venus emerging from the sea.

question_en: Who painted 'The Scream'?
context_en: Edvard Munch painted 'The Scream' in 1893. The Norwegian said the inspiration came when he saw a blood-red sky while walking. It is an icon of Expressionism.

question_en: Who composed the Ninth Symphony while deaf?
context_en: Beethoven composed the Ninth Symphony (1824) completely deaf. The 'Ode to Joy' anthem from the fourth movement is the anthem of the European Union.

question_en: Who wrote 'Crime and Punishment'?
context_en: Dostoevsky published 'Crime and Punishment' in 1866. It tells of the murder committed by Raskolnikov and his subsequent psychological torment. It is a pinnacle of Russian literature.

question_en: Who painted 'The Kiss' (1907-1908)?
context_en: Gustav Klimt painted 'The Kiss' between 1907-1908, in his golden phase. It is the most famous painting of the Vienna Secession. It is in the Belvedere in Vienna.

question_en: Who wrote 'In Search of Lost Time'?
context_en: Marcel Proust published 'In Search of Lost Time' in 7 volumes (1913-1927). It is the longest novel in literature, with ~1.5 million words.

question_en: What is Impressionism?
options_en: A movement that depicts reality exactly
A painting movement that captures the visual impression of a moment with loose brushstrokes
An abstract movement of the 20th century
context_en: Impressionism (1860-1880) sought to capture light and the moment with loose brushstrokes and vivid colors. Monet, Renoir and Degas are its leading figures.

question_en: Who painted 'Sunflowers'?
context_en: Van Gogh painted several versions of 'Sunflowers' in 1888-1889 in Arles. One of them sold in 1987 for $39.9 million.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Metamorphosis'?
context_en: Franz Kafka published 'The Metamorphosis' in 1915. It tells how Gregor Samsa wakes up turned into an insect. It is an essential work of the absurd and existentialism.

question_en: Who composed the 'Requiem'?
context_en: Mozart composed the Requiem in 1791, the year of his death, leaving it unfinished. It was completed by his pupil Franz Xaver Süssmayr. It is one of the most mysterious works in music.

question_en: Who was the first director to win the Oscar five times?
context_en: John Ford won 4 Best Director Oscars (a record). William Wyler won 3. The overall biggest Oscar winner is Walt Disney with 22 statuettes.

question_en: Who painted 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon'?
context_en: Picasso painted 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' in 1907, considered the origin of Cubism. It depicts five prostitutes in a brothel in Barcelona.

question_en: Who wrote 'Don Quixote'?
context_en: Cervantes published the first part of Don Quixote in 1605 and the second in 1615. It is the best-selling novel in history after the Bible, with more than 500 million copies.

question_en: Which architects built the Parthenon?
context_en: The Parthenon was designed by the architects Ictinus and Callicrates and decorated by the sculptor Phidias. It was built between 447-438 BC on the Acropolis of Athens.

question_en: Who composed 'The Rite of Spring'?
context_en: Stravinsky composed 'The Rite of Spring' in 1913. Its Paris premiere caused a scandal: the audience booed and fought in the theater over its radical modernism.

question_en: Who wrote 'Anna Karenina'?
context_en: Leo Tolstoy published 'Anna Karenina' between 1875-1877. Along with 'War and Peace', it is considered one of the greatest novels ever written.

question_en: Who painted 'The Persistence of Memory'?
context_en: Dalí painted 'The Persistence of Memory' in 1931. The soft clocks represent the relativity of time. It measures only 24×33 cm and is in the MoMA in New York.

question_en: Who designed the Colosseum in Rome?
context_en: The Colosseum was built by order of Emperor Vespasian (~AD 72) and completed by his son Titus in AD 80. It could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators.

question_en: Who wrote 'Les Misérables'?
context_en: Victor Hugo published 'Les Misérables' in 1862. It tells the story of Jean Valjean and is one of the most adapted novels for film and musical theater.

question_en: Which artistic movement did Frida Kahlo represent?
context_en: Frida Kahlo was associated with Surrealism, although she distanced herself from the movement. Her self-portraits explore physical pain, identity and Mexican culture.

question_en: Who composed the opera 'Carmen'?
context_en: Georges Bizet composed 'Carmen' in 1875. Based on Mérimée's novel, it was a failure at its premiere but today is the most performed opera in the world.

question_en: Who wrote 'The Lord of the Rings'?
context_en: J.R.R. Tolkien published 'The Lord of the Rings' in three volumes between 1954-1955. He also created the world of Middle-earth with 'The Hobbit' (1937).

question_en: Which painter cut off part of his ear in 1888?
context_en: Vincent van Gogh cut off part of his left earlobe in Arles in December 1888, during a psychological crisis.

question_en: Who painted 'The Baptism of Christ' while Leonardo was his pupil?
context_en: Andrea del Verrocchio ran the Florentine workshop where Leonardo trained. Leonardo painted one of the angels in 'The Baptism of Christ'.

question_en: Which Spanish Baroque painter painted 'The Lances'?
context_en: 'The Surrender of Breda' (1635), known as 'The Lances', is one of the masterpieces of Diego Velázquez.

question_en: Which Catalan modernist architect designed Casa Batlló?
context_en: Antoni Gaudí remodeled Casa Batlló (1904-1906) on Passeig de Gràcia in Barcelona. It is a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Who painted 'Liberty Leading the People'?
context_en: Eugène Delacroix painted 'Liberty Leading the People' (1830) to commemorate the July Revolution in France. It is in the Louvre.

question_en: Which Renaissance painter painted the Sistine Chapel?
context_en: Michelangelo painted the ceiling (1508-1512) and 'The Last Judgment' (1536-1541) in the Sistine Chapel of the Vatican.

question_en: Who painted 'The School of Athens'?
context_en: 'The School of Athens' (1509-1511) was painted by Raphael in the Vatican Rooms. It depicts the great Greek philosophers.

question_en: Which artist painted 'Wanderer above the Sea of Fog'?
context_en: Caspar David Friedrich painted this icon of German Romanticism around 1818. A figure contemplates a sublime landscape of fog.

question_en: Which Flemish painter painted 'The Arnolfini Portrait' (1434)?
context_en: Jan van Eyck painted 'The Arnolfini Portrait', a masterpiece of early Flemish painting. It is in the National Gallery in London.

question_en: Which Dutch painter is famous for his urban views of Delft?
context_en: Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) painted 'View of Delft', 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' and other masterpieces of the Dutch Baroque.

question_en: Who painted 'Girl with a Pearl Earring'?
context_en: 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' (~1665) by Johannes Vermeer is kept in the Mauritshuis museum in The Hague.

question_en: Who painted 'The Night Watch'?
context_en: 'The Night Watch' (1642) is a masterpiece by Rembrandt. It is in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

question_en: Which artistic movement did Pablo Picasso found along with Georges Braque?
context_en: Picasso and Braque developed Cubism between 1907 and 1914, fragmenting the representation of reality into geometric planes.

question_en: Who painted 'The Third of May 1808'?
context_en: Francisco de Goya painted this work in 1814, depicting Napoleon's repression in Madrid on 3 May 1808. It is in the Prado.

question_en: Which artistic movement did Claude Monet start?
context_en: Monet was a key figure of Impressionism. His work 'Impression, Sunrise' (1872) gave the movement its name.

question_en: Who sculpted the Vatican 'Pietà'?
context_en: Michelangelo sculpted 'The Pietà' between 1498 and 1499. It is the only sculpture he signed. It is in St. Peter's Basilica.

question_en: Who sculpted 'The Ecstasy of Saint Teresa'?
context_en: Gian Lorenzo Bernini sculpted this work of the Italian Baroque (1647-1652) in the church of Santa Maria della Vittoria, Rome.

question_en: Who painted 'The Spinners'?
context_en: 'The Spinners' (~1657) is a complex work by Diego Velázquez, with spinners in the foreground and the myth of Arachne in the background.

question_en: In which museum is 'Las Meninas' exhibited?
context_en: 'Las Meninas' (1656) by Velázquez is exhibited in the Prado Museum, in Madrid.

question_en: In which museum is 'Guernica'?
context_en: 'Guernica' (1937) by Picasso has been exhibited in the Reina Sofía National Art Museum in Madrid since 1992.

question_en: Which American architect designed Fallingwater?
context_en: Fallingwater was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright in 1935 in Pennsylvania.

question_en: Which Mexican painter painted murals about the revolution?
context_en: Diego Rivera was one of the great Mexican muralists. He painted the history of Mexico in large public murals.

question_en: Who painted 'The Embrace' (1917)?
context_en: Egon Schiele painted 'The Embrace' in 1917. A pupil of Klimt and a central figure of Viennese Expressionism.

question_en: Which architect founded the Bauhaus?
context_en: Walter Gropius founded the Bauhaus in Weimar in 1919. The school moved to Dessau (1925) and to Berlin (1932) before closing.

question_en: Which Swiss-French architect designed Villa Savoye?
context_en: Le Corbusier designed Villa Savoye (1928-1931) in Poissy, a manifesto of the 'five points' of modern architecture.

question_en: Which Italian Renaissance painter is famous for his madonnas?
context_en: Raphael Sanzio painted numerous madonnas, such as the 'Sistine Madonna' or the 'Madonna of the Goldfinch'.

question_en: Which Baroque painter used intense chiaroscuro and was famous for his turbulent life?
context_en: Caravaggio (1571-1610) revolutionized painting with his dramatic chiaroscuro. His life was marked by fights and a murder.

question_en: Who painted 'The Ecstasy of Saint Francis' in Mannerist style?
context_en: El Greco (Doménikos Theotokópoulos, 1541-1614) painted several works on Saint Francis. Settled in Toledo, he fused Byzantine and Venetian traditions.

question_en: Which painter painted the Prado 'Annunciation' (~1426)?
context_en: Fra Angelico painted 'The Annunciation' (~1425-1428), which is kept in the Prado Museum.

question_en: Who sculpted 'The Gates of Hell'?
context_en: Auguste Rodin worked on 'The Gates of Hell' for almost 40 years. Works like 'The Thinker' and 'The Kiss' derive from it.

question_en: Who painted 'Woman with a Parasol'?
context_en: Claude Monet painted several paintings of his wife Camille with a parasol, the most famous in 1875.

question_en: Who painted 'Olympia' (1863)?
context_en: Édouard Manet painted 'Olympia' in 1863, causing a scandal due to its raw realism. It heralds pictorial modernity.

question_en: Who is the author of the book 'Confessions'?
context_en: Saint Augustine of Hippo wrote 'Confessions' around AD 400, a spiritual autobiography.

question_en: Which building did Filippo Brunelleschi design?
context_en: Brunelleschi designed the famous dome of Florence Cathedral (Santa Maria del Fiore), completed in 1436.

question_en: Which architect designed the Sydney Opera House?
context_en: The Dane Jørn Utzon won the competition in 1957. The Sydney Opera House was completed in 1973 and is a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Which Belgian Surrealist painter created 'The Son of Man'?
context_en: René Magritte painted 'The Son of Man' in 1964, a self-portrait with a bowler hat and an apple hiding the face.

question_en: Who painted 'Composition VIII' and pioneered abstract painting?
context_en: Wassily Kandinsky is considered a pioneer of abstract art. 'Composition VIII' (1923) is in the Guggenheim.

question_en: Which artistic movement did Mondrian found?
context_en: Piet Mondrian founded Neoplasticism (De Stijl), based on straight lines and primary colors.

question_en: Which Russian painter founded Suprematism?
context_en: Kazimir Malevich founded Suprematism, a movement of pure geometry. His emblematic work is 'Black Square' (1915).

question_en: Which Italian Baroque architect designed the colonnade of St. Peter's Square?
context_en: Gian Lorenzo Bernini designed the elliptical colonnade of St. Peter's Square (1656-1667) that embraces the faithful.

question_en: Which architect originally designed the dome of St. Peter's?
context_en: Michelangelo designed the current dome of St. Peter's from 1547. It was finished in 1590 after his death.

question_en: In which museum is Botticelli's 'The Birth of Venus'?
context_en: 'The Birth of Venus' (~1485) by Sandro Botticelli is in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence.

question_en: Who painted 'The Triumph of Death'?
context_en: Pieter Bruegel the Elder painted 'The Triumph of Death' around 1562. It is in the Prado Museum.

question_en: What does 'Pop Art' represent?
context_en: Pop Art uses images from popular and mass culture. Its leading exponents were Andy Warhol and Roy Lichtenstein.

question_en: Which artist created 'Campbell's Soup' as a pop icon?
context_en: Andy Warhol created his famous 'Campbell's Soup Cans' in 1962, an icon of Pop Art.

question_en: Which 1960s movement stood out for spontaneous gestures and 'drip painting'?
context_en: Abstract Expressionism had in Jackson Pollock a leading exponent with his 'dripping' technique.

question_en: Who is famous for his 'drip' paintings?
context_en: Jackson Pollock revolutionized painting with his drip technique, letting paint fall onto canvases spread on the floor.

question_en: Which American painter painted blocks of color (red, orange, etc.)?
context_en: Mark Rothko is famous for his paintings of large blurred blocks of color that invite contemplation.

question_en: Who painted 'One and Three Chairs' (conceptual art)?
context_en: Joseph Kosuth made 'One and Three Chairs' in 1965, a key work of conceptual art.

question_en: Who is the author of the Tuileries Garden in Paris (original design)?
context_en: André Le Nôtre, Louis XIV's gardener, redesigned the Tuileries Garden and designed the gardens of Versailles.

question_en: Who painted 'The Descent from the Cross' in Antwerp Cathedral?
context_en: Peter Paul Rubens painted this monumental altarpiece around 1612-1614. It is a pinnacle work of the Flemish Baroque.

question_en: Who is famous for his court portraits in the court of Charles IV?
context_en: Francisco de Goya was court painter to Charles IV. He painted 'The Family of Charles IV' (1800) with astonishing frankness.

question_en: What is Joan Miró's preferred technique?
context_en: Miró developed his own language of symbolic figures, blots and flat colors. Linked to Surrealism and Catalonia.

question_en: Which Catalan painter is associated with the Miró Foundation in Mallorca?
context_en: The Pilar and Joan Miró Foundation in Mallorca preserves the painter's studio and many of his works.

question_en: Which Aragonese Spanish painter is famous for paintings of types and customs?
context_en: Francisco de Goya y Lucientes, born in Fuendetodos (Aragon), painted both court subjects and popular types.

question_en: Which Valencian painter stood out for the Mediterranean light ('luminism')?
context_en: Joaquín Sorolla (1863-1923) stood out for the vibrant light of his Mediterranean beaches. His museum is in Madrid.

question_en: Who painted 'Saturn Devouring His Son'?
context_en: Goya painted 'Saturn Devouring His Son' as part of the 'Black Paintings' (1819-1823) in the Quinta del Sordo.

question_en: Which English painter is famous for his atmospheric seascapes?
context_en: J.M.W. Turner (1775-1851) revolutionized landscape painting with his effects of light and atmosphere. He anticipated Impressionism.

question_en: Who sculpted 'The Kiss' (1882-1889)?
context_en: Auguste Rodin sculpted 'The Kiss' (several versions in marble and bronze) depicting Paolo and Francesca from the Divine Comedy.

question_en: Who painted 'Luncheon on the Grass'?
context_en: Édouard Manet scandalized the Paris Salon in 1863 with 'Le déjeuner sur l'herbe', a work that anticipated Impressionism.

question_en: Which Barcelona modernist architect designed the Hospital de Sant Pau?
context_en: Lluís Domènech i Montaner designed the Hospital de Sant Pau in Barcelona (1902-1930). It is a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Which French literary and artistic movement rejected the rules in 1916?
context_en: Dadaism was born in 1916 at the Cabaret Voltaire in Zurich. It rejected logic, reason and bourgeois aesthetics.

question_en: Who created the 'Fountain' (urinal) of 1917?
context_en: Marcel Duchamp presented 'Fountain' (a urinal signed 'R. Mutt') as a work of art in 1917, questioning the very notion of art.

question_en: Which Chinese-American artist designed the Vietnam Veterans Memorial?
context_en: Maya Lin designed the Vietnam Veterans Memorial in Washington (1982) while still a student.

question_en: Which Chinese-American architect designed the Louvre Pyramid?
context_en: I.M. Pei (Ieoh Ming Pei) designed the famous glass pyramid of the Louvre, inaugurated in 1989.

question_en: Which Italian Renaissance painter painted 'Primavera'?
context_en: 'Primavera' (~1482) by Sandro Botticelli is exhibited in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence.

question_en: Who sculpted a bronze 'David' (15th century, before Michelangelo's)?
context_en: Donatello made his famous bronze 'David' around 1440. It was the first monumental nude since classical antiquity.

question_en: Which 15th-century Dutch painter painted 'The Descent from the Cross' (Prado)?
context_en: Rogier van der Weyden painted 'The Descent from the Cross' (~1435), a pinnacle work of the Prado and of early Flemish painting.

question_en: Who painted the 'Brancacci Chapel' in Florence?
context_en: Masaccio painted the frescoes of the Brancacci Chapel around 1425, a milestone of the early Florentine Renaissance.

question_en: In which city is the Guggenheim Museum designed by Wright?
context_en: The Guggenheim in New York, designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, opened in 1959 with its characteristic interior spiral.

question_en: Which Canadian-American architect designed the Bilbao Guggenheim?
context_en: Frank Gehry designed the Guggenheim Bilbao, inaugurated in 1997. Its titanium form revolutionized architecture.

question_en: Who is the author of the 'Italian Symphony'?
context_en: Felix Mendelssohn composed his 4th Symphony ('Italian') in 1833 after a trip through Italy.

question_en: Which photographer is famous for his black-and-white landscapes of the American West?
context_en: Ansel Adams (1902-1984) captured iconic landscapes of Yosemite National Park and other places in the American West.

question_en: Who is considered the father of modern color photography?
context_en: William Eggleston elevated the use of color in art photography with his pioneering exhibition at the MoMA in 1976.

question_en: In what year was the Eiffel Tower built?
context_en: The Eiffel Tower was inaugurated in 1889 for the Paris World's Fair. Designed by Gustave Eiffel.

question_en: Who is the author of the Statue of Liberty?
context_en: Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi sculpted the Statue of Liberty. The internal structure was by Gustave Eiffel. A gift from France to the U.S. (1886).

question_en: Who painted 'American Gothic' (1930)?
context_en: Grant Wood painted 'American Gothic' in 1930. It depicts a farmer and his daughter in front of a house in Iowa.

question_en: Which American painter is famous for 'Nighthawks' (1942)?
context_en: Edward Hopper painted 'Nighthawks' in 1942, an icon of American urban loneliness.

question_en: Which Mexican painter self-portrayed herself with flowers in her hair?
context_en: Frida Kahlo (1907-1954) painted numerous self-portraits mixing surrealist, indigenous and personal elements.

question_en: Who painted 'The Two Fridas'?
context_en: 'The Two Fridas' (1939) by Frida Kahlo depicts a double image of the artist after her divorce from Diego Rivera.

question_en: Who painted 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' (1907)?
context_en: 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon' by Picasso (1907) marked the beginning of Cubism. It is in the MoMA in New York.

question_en: Which artistic movement did André Breton found in 1924?
context_en: André Breton published the 'Surrealist Manifesto' in 1924, founding this movement linked to the unconscious and dreams.

question_en: Which Catalan Surrealist painter starred in 'Un Chien Andalou' with Buñuel?
context_en: Salvador Dalí collaborated with Luis Buñuel on 'Un Chien Andalou' (1929) and 'L'Age d'Or' (1930).

question_en: Who painted 'The Great Wave' (Japanese woodblock print)?
context_en: 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa' (~1831) is a woodblock print by Katsushika Hokusai, part of the series '36 Views of Mount Fuji'.

question_en: Who painted 'The Nobleman with his Hand on his Chest'?
context_en: El Greco painted 'The Nobleman with his Hand on his Chest' (~1580). It is in the Prado Museum.

question_en: Which Flemish-German painter painted 'Christ on the Cross' with two panels?
context_en: Matthias Grünewald painted the 'Isenheim Altarpiece' (1512-1516), a pinnacle work of the late Gothic with a dramatic Christ on the cross.

question_en: Which Italian Renaissance architect wrote 'The Four Books of Architecture'?
context_en: Andrea Palladio published 'I quattro libri dell'architettura' in 1570. It influenced classical architecture in later centuries.

question_en: Which Spanish Baroque painter painted immaculate virgins?
context_en: Bartolomé Esteban Murillo (1617-1682) painted numerous 'Immaculates' very popular in Baroque Spain.

question_en: Who painted 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters'?
context_en: Goya engraved 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters' as part of 'Los Caprichos' (1797-1799).

question_en: Which Italian architect designed the Capitoline Square in Rome?
context_en: Michelangelo redesigned the Capitoline Square (Piazza del Campidoglio) in Rome from 1538.

question_en: Which musical work did Vivaldi compose in 1725?
context_en: Antonio Vivaldi composed 'Le quattro stagioni' (The Four Seasons) around 1718-1720, published in Amsterdam in 1725.

question_en: Who painted 'The Raft of the Medusa'?
context_en: Théodore Géricault painted 'The Raft of the Medusa' (1818-1819) after the shipwreck of the Méduse. It is in the Louvre.

question_en: Which German Renaissance painter painted 'Knight, Death and the Devil'?
context_en: Albrecht Dürer made this engraving in 1513. It is one of his three 'Master Prints'.

question_en: Who painted 'Girl with Balloon' on the walls of London?
context_en: Banksy, an anonymous British street artist, created 'Girl with Balloon' in 2002, one of his most iconic works.

question_en: Which Valencian architect designed the City of Arts and Sciences?
context_en: Santiago Calatrava designed the City of Arts and Sciences in Valencia (1998-2009).

question_en: Which Dutch Baroque painter made many self-portraits?
context_en: Rembrandt van Rijn painted more than 80 self-portraits throughout his life, documenting his physical and artistic evolution.

question_en: Which Iranian-British architect designed the Heydar Aliyev Center in Baku?
context_en: Zaha Hadid (1950-2016) was the first woman to win the Pritzker Prize (2004). She designed fluid and dynamic buildings.

question_en: Which artistic movement was associated with machines and speed?
context_en: Italian Futurism, founded by Marinetti in 1909, exalted modernity, the machine and speed.

question_en: Which architect led the reconstruction of the Reichstag in Berlin?
context_en: Norman Foster (British) designed the transparent dome of the Reichstag in 1999 after German reunification.

question_en: Who painted 'Two Tahitian Women' (1899)?
context_en: Paul Gauguin painted numerous works during his stay in Tahiti. 'Two Tahitian Women' (1899) is in the Met in New York.

question_en: Who sculpted 'Christ the Redeemer' in Rio de Janeiro?
context_en: Paul Landowski sculpted the Christ the Redeemer of Corcovado, inaugurated in 1931. Chief engineer: Heitor da Silva Costa.

question_en: Which French Impressionist woman painter painted bathers (19th century)?
context_en: Berthe Morisot was one of the few women in the Impressionist core. Her work depicts intimate and feminine life.

question_en: Which Post-Impressionist painter fell out with Gauguin after a fight?
context_en: Van Gogh and Gauguin lived together in Arles in 1888. The relationship ended with Van Gogh's cut-ear episode.

question_en: Who painted 'Bathers at Asnières' (1884)?
context_en: Georges Seurat founded Pointillism. 'Bathers at Asnières' (1884) and 'A Sunday on La Grande Jatte' (1886) are his masterpieces.

question_en: Which Barcelona modernist architect designed the Palau de la Música Catalana?
context_en: Lluís Domènech i Montaner designed the Palau de la Música Catalana (1905-1908), a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Which author wrote 'The Decameron'?
context_en: Giovanni Boccaccio wrote 'Il Decameron' (1351-1353), a collection of 100 tales told by young people fleeing the plague.

question_en: Which Renaissance work did Petrarch write?
context_en: Petrarch wrote the 'Canzoniere', 366 poems largely dedicated to his beloved Laura.

question_en: Who is the author of 'Orlando Furioso'?
context_en: Ludovico Ariosto published 'Orlando Furioso' in 1516. An epic-chivalric poem of the Italian Renaissance.

question_en: Which American painter painted 'Christina's World' (1948)?
context_en: Andrew Wyeth painted 'Christina's World' (1948), one of the icons of 20th-century American realism.

question_en: Who painted 'Marilyn Monroe' in a pop silkscreen?
context_en: Andy Warhol created the 'Marilyn' series in 1962, shortly after the actress's death, reproducing her image in vivid colors.
